sentences.text: Right.
Yes.
Camp Vught was probably the worst camp for me, because I was separated from my brother for a short time.
We arrived there.
We were still very well nourished from all of that good food we had eaten at the convent.
And so at first I couldn't eat at all, the food there.
But what was so dreadful for me was the fact that I was placed in the women's camp, and George was placed with the men.
And the separation was just totally devastating for me.
It was worse than the experience at Bergen-Belsen because I felt totally isolated, forlorn, abandoned.
And I cried all the time.
I couldn't eat the food.
And I couldn't understand why the other people weren't crying.
I thought there was something wrong with them, that they were not crying.
But on Sundays, George would come to visit me, and I would be so happy then.
What I remember about Vught, initially, was that we had to take off our clothes and put them on a big pile.
And they gave us something to wear, but it was so windy at that time, that I could feel the sand just scratching my legs.
And then I did find my dress again, which I was very happy about.
Evidently, it was sent to be deloused.
At Camp Vught, shortly after I got there, I had one of the most joyful experiences also because May 11th is my birthday.
It was my eighth birthday.
And we were called to the commandant's office.
Now, that scared me a lot because the guards there, the Nazi guards, men as well as women, were very brutal.
And they would hit people.
And when we'd have barrack inspection, for instance, all the beds had to be perfectly made.
And if there was any little piece of lint, any excuse, they would take the-- I guess, the leader of the barrack, and beat, beat the person.
And that was, that was terrible.
And we had to stand at roll call, [? at ?] [? appel, ?] every morning and-- for hours and hours.
And you know, well, at that time, it was still nice and-- fairly nice, springtime.
So it wasn't too cold or too hot.
But still, we had to stand a long time.
There was a woman by the name of Flo Isaak who took me under her wing, and she kind of looked after me.
And so, I wasn't so alone.
But as I was saying, on my eighth birthday we were called to the commandant's office.
And you know, I thought we were going to get beaten or we had done something wrong.
But when we got to the commandant's office, there was Mr.
Van Mackelenbergh sitting in his office.
And I was just so overjoyed that I started to cry.
And another officer, a very cruel officer, Epplinger, he took me and he put me on his lap.
And of course, I was scared to death, you know, to be sitting there.
But on the other hand, I was also afraid to say, I don't want to sit here.
But I had often seen him hitting people, Epplinger.
Well, I forgot about Epplinger because Mr.
Van Mackelenbergh was there.
And he said, you know, he was there for my birthday.
And he brought package with food and chocolates.
And he said he was going to visit us again.
And the reason-- this was highly unusual for any civilian to come into the camp.
What had happened was this.
Mr.
Van Mackelenbergh had a hardware store in [NON-ENGLISH], and he did business dealings with the commandant.
And he finagled somehow, he gave him big discounts and good prices, maybe some things free of charge.
And in that way, he got a foot in the door and was able to visit us at Camp Vught.
Now, when Mr.
Van Mackelenbergh was there, he said to the commandant, you see, you see, he said, those children they don't look Jewish.
Look how fair their skin is, and they have blue eyes.
They have a Catholic father, and he lives in America.
So this put a question mark in the commandant's mind about our background.
Because of that lie, we were saved.
We were saved because of the help we got from the people in Holland, and because of luck, and because of this lie.
Because there were transports from Vught to Auschwitz, first to Westerbork and then to Auschwitz.
But if we had gone to Auschwitz, like most of the children, most of the people, we would have been killed right away because of our age.
But instead, because now there was a question about our background, and about, perhaps, our father being in America, we were-- we had a privileged status in the eyes of the Germans.
And we were placed in a barrack with a privileged group of Jews, the diamond cutters of Amsterdam.
They wanted to save the diamond cutters because of their skills, for, for a while, to exploit their skills.
We were placed.
We saw the other transports go, and that was one of my sad experiences in Vught.
You know, when these big transports would go, and the children would go, and you know, the mothers would go, and the father-- all the fathers were somewhere else.
And I would see the people being loaded onto open trucks, and they would sing.
They would sing.
And that amazed me so much.
They would sing a, a Dutch song.
It went-- it was popular at the time-- [NON-ENGLISH], Oh, I wish it were true, then I would have happiness in my life.
And then, they sang the song, [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
And of course, that memory has stayed with me, and it has, it just has been a revelation to me about these people who were there in the camp with us, the adults.
I never had the additional fear that these adults had, about the crematoriums and about what might happen.
I never thought that we would get killed.
I had no idea about the crematorium.
I did not have those fears.
Not from beginning to end did I ever think we would not survive.
But the adults had that additional fear, along with the hunger, the starvation, the, the cold, the heat, the constant itching from lice, the sickness, the diarrhea, the filth, the humiliation.
They had that fear.
I am just amazed at these people, my fellow Jews, because I was there as a child.
I was there from eight to 10 in the concentration camp with absolutely starving people.
They never took my piece of bread from me.
No one ever molested me.
No one ever hit me.
There was always someone who looked after us.
Then in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shortly thereafter, we also were sent to Westerbork.
This is another camp in Holland.
And in Westerbork, we were in an orphanage.
And the orphanage was run by Jewish-- Orthodox Jews.
And now, we had been baptized.
We were raised in the convent.
We had gone to church every Sunday, at least four times.
We were very Catholic, especially my brother.
In this orphanage, we, we went to school during the day.
They had classes for us in mathematics and Hebrew.
And on Friday evenings, they would have a Sabbath celebration.
And at-- and I liked it so much because it was festive, and the people, we sang songs.
And of course, I loved to sing.
And we said prayers.
And because of the festivities, it looked like there was more food too.
My brother, George, had a hard time because he had to wear a yarmulke.
He didn't want to wear a yarmulke.
And that worried me because I thought he wouldn't get anything to eat, and then-- [LAUGHS] I would have to share my food with him.
He also objected when I would recite the prayers, the Jewish prayers.
But it was there in Westerbork that I became conscious of my Jewish background.
And it was not, not something that I consciously thought about.
I just thought about, well, it was nice to pray.
It was nice to sing, You know, to see-- to have some schooling.
The people were nice.
When we first came there, we got a slice of bread and butter.
And it was much better for me, I thought, than Vught.
George was with the boys, but we came together at meal time.
And I knew we were in the same barrack.
It was the, the orphanage barrack.
It was a barrack that was for, for orphans.
It was inside the camp.
Right.
The people in charge there, the people who were in charge.
I don't know their exact names.
But there was a man there, a Mr.-- well, I have his, his name here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a man who knew Mr.
Van Mackelenbergh, and he wrote a letter to Mr.
Van Mackelenbergh in October of 1943.
No, of-- yeah, of '43.
In October of 1943, this-- Mr.
Worman was his name.
He wrote a letter to Mr.
Van Mackelenbergh that he is very concerned about George and me because he has the feeling that we are going to be deported from Westerbork.
That meant to a death camp.
And so, sure enough, in January of 1944, we too left Westerbork.
But instead of being transported to Auschwitz or Sobibor, where we would have been killed right away, we went to Bergen-Belsen.
From October '43 to February of '44.
So we did, we did go to Bergen-Belsen, on a train.
And it was very cold there.
And I was separated from George.
And we were there with a Mrs.
Melkman.
And Mrs.
Melkman was the wife of Mr.
Melkman, who is now in, in Jerusalem.
He is a famous journalist.
She looked after me in Bergen-Belsen, Mrs.
Melkman.
At first, George and I were in separate barracks, but we saw each other.
We were in the-- a section of Bergen-Belsen that is called the, the star camp.
In this camp, we had a few privileges, which made a big difference.
Like I said, we had a certain status because there was a question about our heritage, our background.
And in this camp, there were people who may have been citizens of countries that were with the Allies, the Allied countries.
And so, those people had special status because the Germans thought perhaps they could use some of these people in exchange for hostages.
So the, the star camp, we starved just as much, but we were able to keep our own clothes, and the-- which was very important.
You know, to walk around with, with that striped suit, that totally de-personalizes.
So our own rags were still something that we treasured.
Also, the fact that the families could stay together.
Now, I was with George.
And later on, I was in the same barrack with George, in the mens' barrack.
We were together.
And of course, the conditions in Bergen-Belsen were indescribable.
We got some, some very sweet tea or coffee in the mornings.
And then a little rutabaga soup, just water, at Noon.
And then in the evening, we got the treasured piece of bread.
And like I say, it would have been very easy to snatch that piece of bread from me, but no one ever did.
And I find that very remarkable, considering that I can't ride public transportation in Chicago, or here in Los Angeles, with a little chain around my neck for fear somebody might grab it.
These were starving people, and a crumb of bread meant the difference between life and death.
It would have been very easy to snatch that bread from me.
No one ever did, not my brother, none of-- no one.
I find that-- I just find that remarkable.
Well, people always ask me that.
What did you do there?
We didn't do much.
We stood [? on ?] [? appel. ?] That, that's, that was-- took up most of the day, standing [? at ?] [? appel. ?] Standing in roll call, at roll call, in lines of 5 or 10.
And we stood there for hours and hours.
And we didn't, we didn't work.
We-- I never got hit.
I never got hit.
I didn't do anything.
I vegetated, to tell you the truth.
What I remember about Bergen-Belsen, and I think that this can't rest.
At least, it gives me an idea what conditions were like.
I was so impressed with a group of women.
They were French women.
They were, they said they were the wives French officers who came into the camp after we had been there already a while.
And they were so animated and so full of life to me that I would just sit there and watch them, because they looked so beautiful.
And they put on makeup in the morning.
And they sang songs, French songs.
So as a child, I thought there was something special about the French people, you know?
Because I remember these, these French women.
And when I went back to the Bergen-Belsen memorial site in 1991, I spoke to the director of that camp, Dr.
Thomas Raha.
I told him about these French women.
And he said, yes.
Another woman, Renata la Coeur, wrote a journal in Bergen-Belsen.
She mentioned these French women, and that, in order to show their resistance and their rebellion to the Germans, they would dress in red, white, and blue colors, the colors of the French flag.
And-- but they were so fabulous to me because the other people there looked like-- I didn't know if they were living or dead.
They just stared ahead of them.
The movements became so slow.
The speech became so slow.
And a lot of people just sank to the ground from weakness, starvation, sickness.
Or they would lay on the ground with their clothes just crawling with lice.
Every day, I saw open wagons piled high with corpses, with just the arms and the legs dangling over the side, without any dignity.
Of course, we would be stripped of all of our dignity, in life as well as in death.
But it was a frightful sight to see every day.
And that is what I remember about Bergen-Belsen.
It was just unbelievable, the starvation.
I don't think so.
I don't think so.
But because they came to the camp still nourished, well nourished, they, they still were healthy.
Whereas, we, we had lost our energy.
We had no strength.
The little energy we had, we conserved, probably, by just all of the functions of the body slowed down so much.
The speech, the, the movement.
Everything slowed down, or it seemed like that to me, in contrast to these women who seemed so animated.
You know, who had expression, and who still dressed, and who cared about how they looked, and combed their hair, and made up.
And they had some spirit yet.
I don't know how that happened.
I really don't know how it happened.
We were just put together.
And it was a man's barrack.
We slept in the same bed, and it was very hard for him because I wet the bed.
And the conditions were just abominable.
There were two men who slept in the bunk upstairs from us, and they had just a bucket that they used.
And sometimes it would, you know, overflow.
And it was just indescribable.
What stands out about Bergen-Belsen is the, the hunger.
We were only talking about food.
You know, people ask me, well, what did you think about?
Potatoes.
Only potatoes.
And we thought about getting out.
We always had hope that we would get out.
And I think that that helped us survive.
As. as for me, like I say, I can't speak for my brother.
But as for me, I never doubted that we would not survive.
And-- but we only thought, my only thought, was about potatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] George did say to me that, you know, if I hadn't been there, he would be able, he would have been able to escape.
He, he thought, you know, that without me there, he would have been able to escape.
I doubt that very much.
But, you know, he was, at that time, 14 years old.
And you know, perhaps he thought he could have escaped.
But there were armed guards within-- everywhere, surrounding the camp.
There was electrified barbed wire all around us, two feet deep.
There was no way that we could have escaped.
Then, in the spring of 1945, I remember that we had to line up.
And we were going to get an injection in our arm, like an immunization.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I overheard people say that this is an immunization against typhoid.
Now, you know typhoid and typhus was just raging throughout the camp.
Diarrhea, and people were dying left and right.
And so, we lined up, and they injected us with that.
Of course, there was-- there still is no inoculation against typhoid or typhus.
And pretty soon thereafter, we were put on a train.
And this was April 10th now.
We were put on a train.
We were on that train 13 days, just circling around.
Because at this time now, it was-- the end of the war was coming close.
And the Americans and the British were coming from the west.
The Russians were coming from the east, and the train could not go through.
And we got nothing to eat on the train, and the train would stop, sometimes for days.
And we would go out.
And we would have been able to escape, but we never thought of, of escaping.
This is how we had lost the ability to plan, to reason.
We just always went back to the, to the train.
And I remember one time, we were in the middle of a forest, and it was so beautiful, so green.
And there was a sparkling brook that was just rushing through the forest.
And it just-- you know, it was like a reawakening, a rebirth, spring time.
And it felt so wonderful.
But we always went back, and we just drank whatever puddles there were on the ground.
And I don't know what we ate, because I don't remember eating.
But one morning we woke up, and we heard people shouting outside.
We are free.
We are free.
And nobody paid much attention to it.
But then, we looked outside and we saw Russian soldiers taking the German guards, the few German guards who were still on the train, prisoners.
And we knew that the Russians had overtaken the, the area and had seized the train.
Most of the Germans already had fled the train.
And then, the Russians didn't know what to make of us, I think.
But they looked inside, and then they realized who we were.
And they took all of the corpses out of the train because most of the people had died.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they put the corpses along the train track.
And it was very sad because my brother always sought out Catholic people in the camp.
And there was a Catholic lady that he always talked with.
And she was one of the corpses laying there on the ground.
Mhm.
Mhm.
Mhm.
